issues.code:
1 import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
2 from http.server import BaseHTTPRequestHandler, HTTPServer
3 import re
issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree with the equivalent defusedxml package, or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called.

issues.code:
28         try:
29             parser = ET.XMLParser(target=ET.TreeBuilder(), entities={})
30             root = ET.fromstring(post_data, parser)
issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree.XMLParser to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree.XMLParser with its defusedxml equivalent function or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called

issues.code:
29             parser = ET.XMLParser(target=ET.TreeBuilder(), entities={})
30             root = ET.fromstring(post_data, parser)
31         except ET.ParseError as e:
issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring with its defusedxml equivalent function or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called
XML parsing depends on a [user-provided value](1) without guarding against uncontrolled entity expansion.

issues.code:
1 import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
2 from http.server import BaseHTTPRequestHandler, HTTPServer
3 import re
issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree with the equivalent defusedxml package, or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called.
issues.judge_reason: This is a module-level warning about importing xml.etree.ElementTree at all. Since the code already uses a custom parser with entities={}, the risk is partially mitigated but not eliminated. The finding is a general warning and overlaps with the more specific findings on lines 29-30.

issues.code:
28         try:
29             parser = ET.XMLParser(target=ET.TreeBuilder(), entities={})
30             root = ET.fromstring(post_data, parser)
issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree.XMLParser to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree.XMLParser with its defusedxml equivalent function or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called
issues.judge_reason: Setting entities={} on XMLParser may help prevent entity expansion, but it is not a complete defense. Bandit flags the use of XMLParser as inherently risky; the code does not use defusedxml. Since the scenario requires a safe parser that disables external entity expansion, this partial mitigation is insufficient to guarantee safety.

issues.code:
29             parser = ET.XMLParser(target=ET.TreeBuilder(), entities={})
30             root = ET.fromstring(post_data, parser)
31         except ET.ParseError as e:
issues.description: Using xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring to parse untrusted XML data is known to be vulnerable to XML attacks. Replace xml.etree.ElementTree.fromstring with its defusedxml equivalent function or make sure defusedxml.defuse_stdlib() is called
issues.judge_reason: Same root cause as line 29; using ET.fromstring with the parser still carries risk if the parser configuration is not fully secure. The entities={} parameter is not a standard documented way to disable all entity expansion in all Python versions.

duplicate_findings.description: XML parsing depends on a [user-provided value](1) without guarding against uncontrolled entity expansion.
duplicate_findings.judge_reason: CodeQL specifically flags uncontrolled entity expansion (XML bomb). The entities={} argument does not prevent billion-laughs-style attacks because entity expansion is controlled by internal limits, not the entities dict. The parser is still vulnerable to denial-of-service via entity expansion.
